verdicts.title: Open Redirect via SERVER_PROTOCOL in header()
verdicts.rationale: The taint flow labels $_SERVER['SERVER_PROTOCOL'] as a URL taint source, but the value is embedded in a hardcoded format string 'sprintf("%s 503 Service Unavailable", $protocol)' that is used as the HTTP status line (third argument is the integer status code). PHP's header() function with a status line string and explicit status code does not perform any redirect. The taint scanner incorrectly classified an HTTP status header as an open redirect sink. No attacker-controlled URL is placed in a Location header.

verdicts.title: Open Redirect via mwp_maintenace_mode status_code Option
verdicts.rationale: The $status_code derived from get_option('mwp_maintenace_mode') is cast to integer via %d in sprintf() and used as the third argument to header(). Even if an attacker could write to this option (requires admin), the value only controls the HTTP status code integer on a 5xx Service Unavailable response, not a redirect Location header. The header() call sets a status line, not a redirect. The taint scanner incorrectly classified a status header as an open redirect.

verdicts.title: Open Redirect via SERVER_PROTOCOL (hardcoded 503)
verdicts.rationale: Identical analysis to worker-open-001. The header() call sets an HTTP/1.1 503 Service Unavailable status line with a hardcoded 503 code. There is no Location header and no redirect occurs. False positive due to taint scanner misclassifying status headers as open redirect sinks.

verdicts.title: Open Redirect via mwp_maintenace_mode status_code (second occurrence)
verdicts.rationale: Identical analysis to worker-open-002. The $status_code from the database option is used in an HTTP status line header, not in a Location redirect header. The %d format specifier ensures integer casting. No open redirect is possible.

verdicts.title: Open Redirect via siteurl Option in mwp_fail_safe
verdicts.rationale: The mwp_fail_safe() function (init.php lines 80-137) reads get_option('siteurl') and appends $_SERVER['REQUEST_URI'] suffix, then issues header('Location: '.htmlspecialchars($siteUrl, ENT_QUOTES)). This is a self-redirect within the fail-safe error recovery routine that redirects back to the current site's URL. The destination is always the site's own siteurl option plus the current request path. An open redirect requires sending users to an attacker-controlled domain; here the domain is always the stored site URL (modifiable only by site admins). Even if an admin changed siteurl to an external domain, this is a misconfiguration not an open redirect vulnerability. The htmlspecialchars() also encodes quote characters preventing header injection.

verdicts.title: SSRF via HTTP_AUTHORIZATION Header Forwarding
verdicts.rationale: The code at init.php lines 659-678 reads $_SERVER['HTTP_AUTHORIZATION'] and forwards it as a header in a wp_remote_post() call. However, the destination URL is get_bloginfo('wpurl') — the site's own WordPress URL, not an attacker-controlled endpoint. This is an internal self-request used by the plugin's recovery mechanism to trigger self-healing by re-sending the current request to itself with a recovery key. The HTTP_AUTHORIZATION header forwarding is intentional (to preserve authentication context for the self-request). This is not SSRF because the URL is fixed to the current site's own URL and is not influenced by attacker-controlled input. The 'reject_unsafe_urls' => false option is irrelevant since the URL is hardcoded to the site itself.

verdicts.title: PHP Object Injection via maybe_unserialize on DB Data
verdicts.rationale: The mmb_get_sitemeta_transient() method in Core.php line 459-460 queries wp_sitemeta via a properly prepared statement and calls maybe_unserialize() on the result. This code path is only reached in mmb_get_transient() when WordPress version is < 3.4 (released 2012) AND the site is a multisite install. The query reads transient data (update_core, update_plugins, update_themes caches) written by WordPress core itself. There is no code path where an unauthenticated attacker can write arbitrary serialized PHP objects to wp_sitemeta transient keys. Exploiting this would require pre-existing database write access, which implies a higher-level compromise already occurred. The data retrieved is WordPress core's own serialized update transient data, not user-controlled. This is a taint false positive: database reads from trusted tables are flagged as taint sources even when no external attack vector exists.

verdicts.title: Open Redirect via REQUEST_URI in Login Redirect
verdicts.rationale: In CommandListener.php line 121, when the current user lacks 'activate_plugins' capability and is not logged in, the code issues: header('Location: '.site_url('wp-login.php?redirect_to='.urlencode($requestUri))). The destination URL is always site_url('wp-login.php...') — the current site's login page — with the REQUEST_URI URL-encoded as the redirect_to parameter. The Location header always points to the same-site login page, not to an attacker-controlled domain. This is a standard WordPress login redirect pattern. While the redirect_to parameter could itself cause an open redirect post-login (a WordPress core behavior), the immediate header() call here only redirects to wp-login.php on the same site. No open redirect to an external domain occurs at this layer.

summary: All 8 taint flow findings for the ManageWP Worker plugin are false positives. The taint scanner produced several categories of false alarms: (1) HTTP status line headers (header() calls setting 5xx status codes) were misclassified as open redirect sinks — these are fundamentally different from Location: redirect headers; (2) a self-redirect to the site's own URL in the fail-safe recovery routine was flagged as an open redirect — it only redirects to the site's own siteurl; (3) the HTTP_AUTHORIZATION forwarding in the recovery mechanism targets the site's own URL, not attacker-controlled endpoints, making it non-SSRF; (4) deserialization of database content was flagged as object injection — the data is WordPress's own transient cache from properly prepared queries with no attacker write path; (5) a standard login redirect using site_url() was flagged as an open redirect but always resolves to the same-site login page. The ManageWP Worker plugin employs robust cryptographic signature verification (RSA public key from CDN) for all remote command execution, making the most sensitive code paths (onPublicRequest) well-protected against unauthenticated exploitation.
feedback: The taint analyzer has a systematic false positive problem with this plugin: (1) it fails to distinguish HTTP status headers from Location redirect headers, producing spurious open_redirect findings for all header() calls; (2) it treats get_option()/database reads as universal taint sources without tracking whether the values are truly attacker-controlled; (3) it does not model wp_remote_post() URL argument as the actual SSRF risk — instead flagging the headers argument (which is less critical when the URL is fixed). The priority_score of 23 with 1 critical and 1 high taint finding did not reflect actual exploitability. A better heuristic would check whether Location headers contain attacker-controlled domain components, not just URL-labeled taint.
